content.full_text:
September 21, 1977


Mr. James P. Jackson
City Attorney
Department of Law
812 Tenth Street, Suite 201
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Mr. Jackson:

     This is in response to your request for informal advice dated September 12. The staff of the Commission has reviewed your letter and discussed the issues raised therein, and agrees that the following advice should be issued pursuant to Government Code Section 83114 and 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18329(c)(1).

     We agree with your conclusion that pursuant to the conflict of interest codes adopted by the Civil Service Board and the Retirement Hearing Commission, Mr. Yee should report income received from the city by virtue of the activities described in paragraph one on page one of your letter. His source of income with respect to the activities described in paragraphs two and three, however, is the applicant or prime contractor, not the city. Thus, Mr. Yee should disclose the names of such prime contractors on his Statement of Economic Interests if either of the applicable conflict of interest codes require disclosure of income of this type and if the appropriate monetary thresholds are met.

     We further agree with the tentative conclusion reached in your letter that Mr. Yee need not disqualify himself in any wholesale fashion from participating in Civil Service Board and Retirement Hearing Commission matters because of his relationship with the city described in paragraph one. We conclude this because it does not appear that the activities of these Boards in which Mr. Yee participates will "foreseeably have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally ..." on the city. Government Code Section 87302.

     The Commission's regulation, 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702, defining "material financial effect" lists both a general rule and certain specific criteria to be

Mr. James P. Jackson
September 21, 1977
Page Two

considered in determining the presence of "materiality."
The general rule is as follows:

     (a) The financial effect of a governmental de-
cision on a financial interest of a public official
is material if, at the time the official makes,
participates in making or attempts to use his or
her official position to influence the making of
the decision, in light of all the circumstances
and facts known at the time of the decision, the
official knows or has reason to know that the
existence of the financial interest might inter-
fere with the official's performance of his or her
duties in an impartial manner free from bias.

                              Section 18702(a)

     In addition, the regulation provides that "consideration
should be given, but not limited to" an analysis of (1)
whether the effect of the decision on an official's source
of income meets certain monetary threshold; (2) whether the
decision will directly affect the amount of income to be
received by the official; and (3) whether there is a nexus
between the decision and the purpose for which the official
received income. 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702(b)(3).

     In most cases, if the monetary thresholds set
forth in the regulation are met, materiality will be present.
In the case at hand, however, Mr. Yee's income from the city
will not be affected by the decisions he makes, nor is there
any relationship between these decisions and the engineering
services he provides under contract to the city. Moreover,
it is hard to imagine that Mr. Yee will be biased in favor
of the city's interests simply because he has received money
from the city under a contract which is totally unrelated to
his official duties. We conclude, therefore, that even if
the monetary thresholds set forth in the regulation are met,
the materiality required for disqualification will generally
not be present when Mr. Yee makes decisions that could fore-
seeably have a financial effect on the City of Sacramento.

     It is possible, of course, that the Civil Service
Board and the Retirement Hearing Commission may be called
upon to make decisions that could affect the prime contractors
that have been sources of income to Mr. Yee's firm. Such

Mr. James P. Jackson
September 21, 1977
Page Three


circumstances could require disqualification of Mr. Yee
independent of the effect of these decisions on the city.

     Thank you for writing. I trust this answers your
questions. If I can be of further assistance in this or any
other matter please do not hesitate to call on me at 322-
6444.

                                        Sincerely


                                        Kenneth W. Goshorn
                                        Research Specialist III
                                        Conflict of Interest Division

KWG:plh

CITY OF SACRAMENTO

JAMES P. JACKSON
CITY ATTORNEY

THEODORE H. KOBEY, JR.
ASSISTANT CITY ATTORNEY

DEPARTMENT OF LAW
812 TENTH ST.
SUITE 201

SACRAMENTO, CALIF. 95814

TELEPHONE (916) 449-5348

LELAND J. SAVAGE
TERENCE M. BROWN
ELIZABETH HASSARD SILVER
S. RUSSELL SELIX, JR.
DEPUTY CITY ATTORNEYS

September 12, 1977

Kenneth W. Goshorn, Esq.
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, CA  95814

Dear Ken:

In earlier discussions with you, I indicated that I would write
you a letter concerning the application of proposed conflict of
interest codes to a Sacramento City official. You stated that
you could give me an informal reply. The public official
involved is Mr. James Yee, who is a member of the Civil Service
Board and the Retirement Hearing Commission. These boards hear
cases involving appeals by City employees on matters of
discipline and disability retirement.

The conflict of interest codes for these boards provide that
the board members will disclose sources of income from the City
of Sacramento.

Mr. Yee is a partner of one of the largest engineering firms in
the area and has the following connection with the City of
Sacramento:

1. On occasion the City of Sacramento hires Mr. Yee's
firm to do work for the City. It is my understanding that the
firm has not been hired during the current year, but in the
past Mr. Yee's proportion of the source of income from the City
may be between $1,000 and $2,000 per year.

2. Mr. Yee's firm is also on a City-approved list of
engineering firms authorized to review building plans for
larger structures to be built in the City. Normally a person
pays a building permit fee and the plans are reviewed by the
City. However, where a major building is involved, this review
can take many months. In order to expedite the review process,
the City allows a builder to have the plans checked by a City-
approved engineering firm. The builder pays this engineering
firm directly for its review services. No money passes between
the City and the engineering firm. There are about five or six
engineering firms on the approved list. The builder can go to
any one of several firms. Not every firm in town is on the

Kenneth W. Goshorn, Esq.
September 12, 1977
Page two


approved list, however, since the City wants these approved
firms to be sufficiently qualified to adequately check the
plans.

     3.  On occasion a construction firm doing work for the
City of Sacramento will hire Mr. Yee's engineering firm to
assist it. The engineering firm is a subcontractor. There is
no direct relationship between the City and the firm, nor does
any money pass between the two parties.

     I assume that any money paid Mr. Yee's firm under paragraph
number one, above, would need to be reported as required by the
conflict of interest code.

     My questions are as follows:

     1.  Does money paid Mr. Yee's firm under paragraph numbers
two and three need to be reported under the conflict of
interest code?

     2.  Does Mr. Yee need to disqualify himself at the Civil
Service Board and Retirement Commission meetings because of his
connection with the City?

     It should be understood that if he must disqualify himself, he
may as well not be serving on the boards since the City is
involved in all board actions.

     I have informally advised Mr. Yee that I did not think he needs
to disqualify himself because it is not foreseeable that any of
his actions as a member of the City boards will have any
material effect on his income as a result of his business
relationships with the City.

     Also, I have advised him that he would not have to disclose
income received under paragraph number three, above. I am not
sure how income received under paragraph number two, above,
should be treated.

     I would appreciate your comments on this matter.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        JAMES P. JACKSON, City Attorney
JPJ/pl

sections.conclusion: We agree with your conclusion that pursuant to the conflict of interest codes adopted by the Civil Service Board and the Retirement Hearing Commission, Mr. Yee should report income received from the City by virtue of the activities described in paragraph one. His source of income with respect to the activities described in paragraphs two and three, however, is the applicant or prime contractor, not the City. Thus, Mr. Yee should disclose the names of such prime contractors on his statement of Economic Interests if either of the applicable conflict of interest codes require disclosure of income of this type and if the appropriate monetary thresholds are met. We further agree that Mr. Yee need not disqualify himself in any wholesale fashion from participating in Civil Service Board and Retirement Hearing Commission matters because the activities of these Boards will not foreseeably have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the City.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether a public official serving on the Civil Service Board and Retirement Hearing Commission must disclose income from private engineering work with the City and its contractors, and whether he must disqualify himself from board decisions due to his business relationships with the City.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Mr. Yee must report income directly from the City under paragraph one, but income from prime contractors in paragraphs two and three need only be disclosed if required by the applicable conflict of interest codes and monetary thresholds are met. Mr. Yee generally need not disqualify himself from board matters as his official decisions will not foreseeably have a material financial effect on him, though disqualification may be required in specific cases involving his prime contractor sources of income.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Document includes both the incoming request letter (dated September 12, 1977) and the FPPC response letter (dated September 21, 1977). OCR quality is poor but text is generally legible. The document contains full question and conclusion sections. Minor OCR errors in names ('lree' for 'Yee', 'oii1c1a1' for 'official', etc.) were corrected in extraction.

CONCLUSION: We agree with your conclusion that pursuant to the conflict of interest codes adopted by the Civil Service Board and the Retirement Hearing Commission, Mr. Yee should report income received from the City by virtue of the activities described in paragraph one. His source of income with respect to the activities described in paragraphs two and three, however, is the applicant or prime contractor, not the City. Thus, Mr. Yee should disclose the names of such prime contractors on his statement of Economic Interests if either of the applicable conflict of interest codes require disclosure of income of this type and if the appropriate monetary thresholds are met. We further agree that Mr. Yee need not disqualify himself in any wholesale fashion from participating in Civil Service Board and Retirement Hearing Commission matters because the activities of these Boards will not foreseeably have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the City.

embedding.summary: The FPPC advises City Attorney James Jackson regarding conflict of interest reporting and disqualification requirements for Mr. James Yee, a member of the Civil Service Board and Retirement Hearing Commission who also operates an engineering firm with business relationships with the City. The Commission concludes that Mr. Yee must report direct City income but need not generally disqualify himself despite the City's involvement in board actions.
